stem: What is the mean of \(4, 8, 6, 10, 12\)?
What is the median of the data set \(3, 9, 5, 1, 7\)?
solutionStandard: Sorted: \(1, 3, 5, 7, 9\). The middle value is 5.

stem: What is the median of the data set \(2, 4, 4, 6, 9, 11\)?
solutionStandard: With 6 values sorted as \(2,4,4,6,9,11\), the median is the average of the two middle values: \((4+6)/2 = 5\).

stem: The mean of five numbers is 20. If four of the numbers are 15, 18, 22, and 25, what is the fifth number?
solutionStandard: The sum of all five numbers is \(20 \times 5 = 100\). The four known numbers sum to \(15+18+22+25=80\). The fifth number is \(100-80=20\).
The mode is 2 (appears most often). The median (middle value) is 3. Since \(3 > 2\), Quantity B is greater.

stem: A data set has mean 10 and median 10. Which of the following data sets could this describe? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: Check each: (A) mean \(=(8+9+10+11+12)/5=10\), median (middle of sorted list) \(=10\). Works. (B) mean \(=(1+9+10+11+19)/5=10\), median \(=10\). Works. (C) mean \(=(1+2+3+4+40)/5=10\), but sorted the middle value is \(3\), not \(10\) — an outlier can match the mean without matching the median, so this fails. (D) mean \(=(5+5+10+15+15)/5=10\), median \(=10\). Works.

stem: A set of 7 test scores has a mean of 80. If the lowest score, 50, is removed, what is the new mean of the remaining 6 scores?
solutionStandard: Total sum of 7 scores \(= 80 \times 7 = 560\). Removing 50 leaves \(560 - 50 = 510\) over 6 scores. New mean \(= 510/6 = 85\).

stem: A data set consists of 9 distinct positive integers. The distribution is strongly right-skewed (a long tail of large values).
solutionStandard: In a right-skewed distribution, a small number of unusually large values pulls the mean above the median, since the mean is sensitive to outliers while the median is not.

stem: The mean of 10 numbers is 15. When one number is removed, the mean of the remaining 9 numbers is 14. What number was removed?
solutionStandard: Original sum \(= 15 \times 10 = 150\). New sum \(= 14 \times 9 = 126\). Removed number \(= 150 - 126 = 24\).

stem: A class of 30 students has a mean score of 80 on a test. A second class of 20 students has a mean score of 90 on the same test. What is the mean score of both classes combined?
What is the mean of {{a}}, {{a+d}}, {{a+2*d}}, {{a+3*d}}, {{a+4*d}}?
solutionStandard: These five numbers form an evenly spaced (arithmetic) sequence, so their mean equals the middle value: \({{a+2*d}}\).

stem: What is the median of the data set \(3, 17, 8, 12, 5\)?
solutionStandard: Sorted: \(3, 5, 8, 12, 17\). The middle value is 8.

stem: The mean of {{n}} numbers is {{m}}. What is the sum of these numbers?
solutionStandard: Sum \(=\) mean \(\times\) count \(= {{m}} \times {{n}} = {{n*m}}\).

stem: The mean of 6 numbers is 12. If five of the numbers are 8, 10, 14, 16, and 9, what is the sixth number?
solutionStandard: Total sum \(= 12 \times 6 = 72\). The five known numbers sum to \(8+10+14+16+9=57\). Sixth number \(=72-57=15\).

The mode is \({{k}}\) (it appears twice; all others appear once). The mean is \(\dfrac{2({{k}})+({{k+1}})+({{k+5}})+({{k+9}})}{5} = \dfrac{5k+15}{5} = k+3\), which is always 3 more than the mode. Quantity A is greater.

stem: A data set has mean 15 and median 15. Which of the following data sets could this describe? Select all that apply.
solutionStandard: Check each: (A) sum \(=75\), mean \(15\); sorted median \(15\). Works. (B) sum \(=75\), mean \(15\); median \(15\). Works. (C) sum \(=75\), mean \(15\), but sorted median is \(3\) — fails. (D) sum \(=75\), mean \(15\), but sorted median is \(5\) — fails.

stem: The mean of {{n}} numbers is {{m1}}. When one number is removed, the mean of the remaining {{n-1}} numbers is {{m1+d}}. What number was removed?
solutionStandard: Original sum \(= n \times m_1\). New sum \(= (n-1)(m_1+d)\). Removed number \(= n \cdot m_1 - (n-1)(m_1+d) = {{m1-(n-1)*d}}\).

stem: Every number in a data set is increased by 5.
quantityA: The amount by which the mean increases
solutionStandard: Adding a constant \(c\) to every value in a data set increases the mean by exactly \(c\), since the sum increases by \(cn\) and the mean increases by \(cn/n=c\). Here \(c=5\), so the quantities are always equal.

stem: What is the median of the data set \(4, 9, 7, 4, 12, 9, 15, 9\)?
solutionStandard: Sorted: \(4,4,7,9,9,9,12,15\). With 8 values, the median is the average of the 4th and 5th values, which are both \(9\): \((9+9)/2 = 9\).

stem: A data set of {{n}} numbers has a mean of {{m}}. One more number is added, making the mean of all {{n+1}} numbers equal to {{m+e}}. What number was added?
solutionStandard: Original sum \(= n \cdot m\). New sum \(= (n+1)(m+e)\). Added number \(= (n+1)(m+e) - n \cdot m = m + (n+1)e = {{m+(n+1)*e}}\).
